_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against the North American Contact Dermatitis Group 2019-2020 patch test results (DeKoven Dermatitis 2023, PMID 36917520) and 2021-2022 update (Houle Dermatitis 2025, PMID 40274377), Johansen et al J Allergy Clin Immunol 2022 (PMID 35183605), Tramontana Front Med 2023 (PMID 37283623), Esser/Martin Curr Allergy Asthma Rep 2017 (PMID 29129023), Ahlström Contact Dermatitis 2019 nickel review (PMID 31140194), Thyssen Contact Dermatitis 2022 hand eczema guideline (PMID 34971008), Uter J Eur Acad Dermatol Venereol 2020 MI epidemic (PMID 31419348), and Apfelbacher Br J Dermatol 2025 chronic hand eczema prevalence (PMID 39797908). Drug names and doses cross-checked against UpToDate Lexicomp and AAD/ACDS patient education.

h1Title: Allergic Contact Dermatitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Allergic contact dermatitis is a delayed-type (Type IV) hypersensitivity reaction in which T cells primed against a specific chemical produce an itchy, vesicular rash 24-72 hours after re-exposure. It is the second most common form of contact dermatitis after the irritant subtype and accounts for roughly 20% of all occupational skin disease. Around 20% of the general population is sensitized to at least one common allergen — most often nickel, fragrance mix, methylisothiazolinone, or balsam of Peru — and prevalence is rising as cosmetic and industrial exposures multiply. The rash characteristically maps to the site of contact: earlobes from nickel jewellery, eyelids from cosmetics, hands from rubber gloves, feet from leather chromate. Patch testing identifies the culprit with greater than 70% sensitivity, and strict allergen avoidance plus short courses of topical corticosteroids resolve most cases within 2-3 weeks.

keyStats.prevalence: ~20% of the general European population sensitized to at least one allergen (Alinaghi BJD 2019); ACD accounts for 20% of occupational skin disease in the US
keyStats.demographics: Women affected more than men (mainly through cosmetics, jewellery, hair products); pediatric incidence rising in parallel with adults
keyStats.avgAge: Sensitization can occur at any age; clinical disease peaks 20-65 years, with occupational cases concentrated in the first two decades of working life
keyStats.globalCases: Chronic hand eczema (the dominant ACD presentation in adults) affects roughly 5-9% of European adults at any time (Apfelbacher BJD 2025)

definition: Allergic contact dermatitis (ICD-10: L23) is an eczematous skin reaction caused by a T-cell-mediated, delayed-type (Type IV) hypersensitivity response to a low-molecular-weight chemical that penetrates the stratum corneum and binds skin proteins to form an immunogenic hapten-protein complex. The condition develops in two stages. Sensitization occurs on first prolonged or repeated exposure: Langerhans cells and dermal dendritic cells process the hapten and migrate to regional lymph nodes, where they prime allergen-specific CD8+ and CD4+ effector and memory T cells. The elicitation phase begins on re-exposure — usually 24-72 hours later — when the memory T cells return to the skin, release interferon-γ, IL-17, and IL-4/IL-13, and recruit a cellular infiltrate that produces erythema, edema, papules, and vesicles at the contact site. Severity is graded clinically from mild erythema to bullous, oozing dermatitis, and chronicity (lichenification, fissuring) develops with repeated exposure. The condition is staged into acute, subacute, and chronic phases and is managed primarily by dermatologists, with allergists, occupational physicians, and primary care heavily involved. It sits inside the broader contact dermatitis family alongside irritant contact dermatitis (non-immunologic, dose-dependent barrier injury), contact urticaria (immediate IgE-mediated), and photo-contact dermatitis. The shared end pathway is barrier breakdown plus innate immune activation, but the diagnostic and management implications differ — making accurate classification through history and patch testing essential.

typesClassification.description: Sharply demarcated erythema, edema, vesicles, and oozing 24-72 hours after re-exposure to the allergen. Most often follows a clearly identifiable single high-dose contact such as poison ivy, hair dye, or a new cosmetic.
Persistent erythematous, scaly, papular plaques that develop over weeks of ongoing low-grade exposure. Typical of cosmetic, topical-medication, and metal allergies.

Lichenified, fissured, hyperkeratotic plaques from continued exposure over months or years. Hands and feet are the dominant sites. Frequently mistaken for atopic dermatitis or psoriasis until patch testing is performed.

Generalized eczematous, dyshidrotic, or symmetric flexural eruption (baboon syndrome) after systemic exposure to an allergen in a pre-sensitized patient. Common with metals, balsam of Peru, antibiotics, and some plant-derived foods.

Eczematous reaction limited to sun-exposed sites in patients sensitized to a photoallergen activated by UVA — sunscreens (oxybenzone), NSAID gels (ketoprofen), and some fragrances are the leading culprits.

Eczematous reaction on exposed skin — face, eyelids, neck, V of the chest — from airborne allergens such as sesquiterpene lactones (Compositae plants), epoxy resins, or fragrance vapours. Easily mistaken for photo-dermatitis but spares the submental shadow.

primarySymptoms: Intense itch over the contact area, typically beginning 24-72 hours after re-exposure to the allergen and peaking at day 3-4 — the cardinal feature distinguishing ACD from immediate contact urticaria.
Erythematous papules and tiny clear vesicles that may coalesce into oozing, weeping plaques during the acute phase, often with overlying serous crust.
Sharp geographic borders that match the area of allergen contact — a band around the wrist from a watchband, a rectangle on the abdomen from a nickel jeans stud, streaks on the forearm from a plant.
Burning, stinging, or pain in heavily inflamed or fissured areas, especially on the hands, eyelids, lips, and perianal skin where the stratum corneum is thin.
Lichenification — thickened, leathery skin with exaggerated skin markings — over chronically exposed and scratched sites such as the dorsal hands, eyelids, and feet.
Hyperpigmentation or hypopigmentation after inflammation resolves, especially in skin of color, which can persist for months after the active dermatitis has cleared.
Spread of the eruption beyond the original contact site over days (id reaction or autoeczematization), producing scattered papulovesicles on the trunk and limbs even where no allergen touched.
Recurrent flares each time the patient re-encounters the offending allergen, with shorter latency between exposure and eruption as sensitization deepens.
Eyelid swelling and erythema as an isolated presentation — typical of cosmetic, nail-product, and airborne allergies, since the thin eyelid skin reacts to allergens transferred by the fingers.
Hand dermatitis with palmar and finger-web involvement, fissuring, and nail-fold inflammation in occupational cases (healthcare, food service, hairdressing, cleaning).
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent localized itch and faint erythema under a new piece of jewellery, watchband, or belt buckle that improves when the item is removed
Recurrent eyelid puffiness or redness that flares within 1-2 days of changing eye makeup, mascara, or facial moisturizer
Itchy red patches on the dorsal hands or finger webs in a worker recently exposed to detergents, hair dye, rubber gloves, or epoxy resins
Sharply outlined rash in an unusual distribution that maps to an exposure (wedding ring finger, perianal skin from wet wipes, lateral feet from rubber sandals)
Flares of a previously controlled eczematous area whenever a specific cosmetic, topical medication, or workplace product is reintroduced
emergencySigns: Generalized erythroderma involving more than 90% of the body surface with chills, fever, and dehydration — admit for fluid balance and systemic therapy
Spreading honey-coloured crusting, fever, and lymphangitic streaking suggesting Staphylococcus aureus superinfection or cellulitis needing oral or IV antibiotics
Sudden painful clustered punched-out vesicles superimposed on the dermatitis — possible eczema herpeticum, a dermatologic emergency requiring IV acyclovir
Severe periorbital swelling restricting eye opening, photophobia, or visual change — same-day ophthalmology review to exclude secondary keratoconjunctivitis
Stevens-Johnson syndrome–like skin pain, mucosal involvement, and target lesions within 1-8 weeks of starting a new oral or topical drug — rare but life-threatening

causes.cause: Nickel (jewellery, jeans buttons, mobile phones, surgical implants)
causes.description: The single most common contact allergen worldwide. Roughly 17-19% of women and 3% of men patch-tested in the North American Contact Dermatitis Group 2019-2020 series reacted to nickel sulfate. The EU Nickel Directive (1994, tightened 2009) has reduced rates in regulated products but exposure persists from imported and unregulated items.

causes.cause: Fragrance mix I and II, balsam of Peru, hydroperoxides of linalool and limonene
causes.description: Fragrances are the leading cause of cosmetic ACD. Fragrance mix I detects roughly 8-12% of patch-tested patients in NACDG and European registries. Many marketed 'unscented' products still contain masking fragrances, which is why patch testing with the expanded series is needed to identify true culprits.

Preservatives in liquid soaps, cosmetics, household cleaners, water-based paints, and industrial fluids. Reaction rates rose from 1.4% in 2009 to over 13% in some European clinics by 2014 (Uter JEADV 2020). EU and US cosmetic regulations have since tightened limits, but MI remains a top-5 allergen.

Often applied to broken skin, dramatically raising sensitization risk. NACDG positivity rates: neomycin 7-9%, bacitracin 7-9%. Cross-reaction within the aminoglycoside class is common and can complicate later antibiotic choice.

causes.cause: p-Phenylenediamine (PPD) in permanent and semi-permanent hair dye and black henna tattoos
causes.description: PPD is one of the most potent contact sensitizers and a leading cause of severe scalp, face, and neck dermatitis in adults. Black henna temporary tattoos contain extremely high PPD concentrations and can sensitize children within days; cross-reaction with sulfonamide antibiotics and benzocaine is well documented.

The dominant cause of glove-related occupational hand dermatitis in healthcare, food handling, and cleaning. Rubber accelerators are present in latex and synthetic gloves alike; switching to accelerator-free nitrile gloves resolves most cases.

causes.cause: Urushiol (poison ivy, poison oak, poison sumac)
causes.description: An oleoresin from Toxicodendron plants. Roughly 50-75% of US adults are sensitized; re-exposure produces the linear vesicular rash that is the textbook ACD presentation. The allergen persists on clothing, tools, and pet fur for weeks and can be reactivated by smoke inhalation.

Paradoxically, corticosteroid creams themselves cause ACD in 0.5-5% of long-term users; markers are budesonide and tixocortol pivalate on patch testing. Other common topical culprits include lidocaine, ibuprofen and ketoprofen gels, and ophthalmic drops.

riskFactors.description: Women have higher overall ACD prevalence (roughly 1.5-2 fold versus men), largely from cosmetic, jewellery, and hair-product exposures. Nickel sensitization in women in the NACDG 2019-2020 series was 18.3% versus 3.7% in men.

riskFactors.factor: Occupations with chronic wet-work or chemical exposure
riskFactors.description: Healthcare workers, hairdressers, cleaners, food handlers, construction workers, mechanics, and beauticians carry chronic hand eczema rates of 10-30%. Hand dermatitis is responsible for 80-90% of occupational skin disease in most national registries.

riskFactors.factor: Pre-existing atopic dermatitis or impaired skin barrier
riskFactors.description: Filaggrin loss-of-function variants and active atopic eczema raise allergen penetration and ACD risk roughly 1.5-2 fold. Patients with combined atopic and contact dermatitis are over-represented in tertiary referral clinics.

Cumulative exposure across decades raises the probability of crossing the sensitization threshold for any given allergen. NACDG data show roughly 1.5-fold higher positive patch-test rates after age 40.

riskFactors.factor: Personal history of ear or body piercing in childhood
riskFactors.description: Early ear piercing — especially with low-quality nickel-releasing studs — is the strongest single risk factor for adult nickel allergy, raising risk 3-4 fold (Ahlström Contact Dermatitis 2019).

Each additional leave-on product (creams, makeup, perfumes) marginally raises the probability of fragrance, preservative, or surfactant sensitization. Cosmetic-related ACD now accounts for roughly 30% of facial dermatitis in women.
Family clustering of nickel and fragrance allergy is well documented; specific HLA-DRB1 alleles and N-acetyltransferase polymorphisms influence individual sensitization risk.

Over-the-counter triple-antibiotic ointments and ophthalmic preparations applied to broken skin substantially raise sensitization, particularly in older adults with leg ulcers or post-surgical wounds.

riskFactors.factor: Black henna temporary tattoos in childhood or travel
riskFactors.description: Black henna paste contains very high concentrations of p-phenylenediamine (PPD). Even a single application can sensitize a child within 1-2 weeks and produce severe lifelong PPD allergy with hair dye cross-reactions.

riskFactors.factor: Chronic leg ulcers or stasis dermatitis
riskFactors.description: Long-standing barrier disruption plus exposure to topical antibiotics, lanolin, and rubber bandages drives high sensitization rates (40-60% positive patch tests in leg-ulcer clinics).

affectedDemographics: Roughly 20% of the European general population is sensitized to at least one common allergen (Alinaghi BJD 2019 meta-analysis)
ACD accounts for about 20% of all occupational skin disease in the United States; hand dermatitis dominates occupational cases
Women carry roughly 1.5-2 fold higher overall prevalence than men, driven primarily by nickel, fragrance, and cosmetic preservative sensitization
Pediatric ACD is increasingly recognized — about 25-30% of patch-tested children show clinically relevant reactions, with nickel, fragrance, preservatives, and topical antibiotics dominating

Chronic hand eczema (the dominant ACD presentation in adults) affects 5-9% of European adults at any time and up to 40% of workers in wet-work occupations (Thyssen Contact Dermatitis 2022; Apfelbacher BJD 2025)
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of allergic contact dermatitis begins with a detailed exposure history aligned to the rash pattern: when did it start, what touched the skin, what improves it on weekends or holidays, what at-work versus at-home products are used. The decisive investigation is patch testing — the gold-standard diagnostic recommended by both the American Contact Dermatitis Society and the European Society of Contact Dermatitis. The technique applies standardized allergen-loaded chambers to the upper back for 48 hours, with readings at day 2, day 4 and (where logistics allow) day 7. The North American 80-allergen Core Series and the European Baseline Series each detect 70-80% of clinically relevant allergens; supplemental series (cosmetics, hairdressers, dental, plastics-and-glues, fragrance, plants) are added based on history. A positive reaction grades from doubtful (?+) through 1+ erythema and papules, 2+ vesicles, to 3+ bullous reactions; relevance to the current dermatitis must then be assessed by an experienced reader using the patient's exposures. Histology is not specific but can exclude mimickers in atypical cases — biopsy of acute ACD shows spongiosis and a perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate, similar to atopic dermatitis. Specific IgE and skin-prick tests have no role in classical ACD, since the disease is T-cell mediated rather than IgE mediated; they are reserved for suspected contact urticaria. The decisive differentials to keep in mind are irritant contact dermatitis (non-immunologic, more burning than itching, no sharp borders), atopic dermatitis (flexural, lifelong, atopic stigmata), nummular eczema (coin-shaped, no allergen pattern), seborrheic dermatitis (greasy scale on sebum-rich areas), psoriasis (well-demarcated plaques with silvery scale), and tinea (KOH-positive scale, central clearing). A negative comprehensive patch test in a true ACD-pattern eruption should prompt re-testing with expanded or work-specific series and review of patients' own products before concluding the disease is not allergic.

diagnosticTests.test: Patch testing (Core Series or European Baseline Series)
diagnosticTests.purpose: The reference standard. Identifies delayed-type hypersensitivity to a panel of 70-80 standardized allergens covering the most common metals, fragrances, preservatives, rubber chemicals, hair dyes, topical medications, and resins. Sensitivity 70-80% with a comprehensive series; supplemental series raise detection further.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Allergen-loaded chambers are taped to the upper back. Patients keep the area dry, avoid sweating, exercise, and sun exposure, and return at 48 hours for chamber removal and an initial read, then again at 72-96 hours and ideally 5-7 days for delayed reactions. Each session takes 30-60 minutes. The procedure is painless; some patients feel mild itch over true positives.

diagnosticTests.test: Repeat open application test (ROAT) or use test
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms clinical relevance when a patient's own product produces a doubtful or low-grade patch-test reaction. The product is applied twice daily to a small area of the antecubital fossa for 7-21 days to see if dermatitis develops.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patients self-apply a thin film of the product to a 2 cm × 2 cm area twice a day and monitor for itch, redness, or papules. A positive ROAT supports causation; a negative test argues against the product being relevant.

diagnosticTests.test: Detailed exposure history and work-environment review
diagnosticTests.purpose: Often diagnostic on its own when distribution maps to a specific contact (earlobes-nickel earrings, eyelids-cosmetics, hands-gloves, feet-leather chromate, perianal-wet wipes). Required for selecting supplemental patch-test series.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-60 minute structured interview covering hobbies, occupation, cosmetics, jewellery, topical medications, household products, plants, and recent travel. Patients are asked to bring their own products and ingredient lists to the appointment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for atypical or treatment-refractory cases to exclude cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, psoriasis, lichen planus, or pemphigoid. Histology of ACD shows spongiosis with perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate but cannot reliably distinguish ACD from irritant contact dermatitis or atopic dermatitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 4 mm punch biopsy under local anaesthetic, with 1-2 sutures, takes 10-15 minutes in clinic. Mild post-biopsy soreness and a small scar are expected; results return in 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: Wood's lamp and KOH microscopy of skin scrapings
diagnosticTests.purpose: Exclude tinea corporis (KOH-positive hyphae, central clearing on Wood's lamp) and erythrasma when the morphology is ambiguous. Both can mimic chronic ACD of the groin, feet, or axillae.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless. Skin is gently scraped with a blade, the sample mounted with 10-20% potassium hydroxide and examined under a microscope; the Wood's lamp is held over the lesion for 30-60 seconds in a darkened room.

diagnosticTests.test: Bacterial swab and culture (when superinfection suspected)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies Staphylococcus aureus impetiginization in oozing, crusted lesions and guides antibiotic choice. Methicillin-resistant strains are detected on routine culture.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A sterile swab is rolled over the lesion for a few seconds; culture results return in 24-72 hours.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of allergic contact dermatitis follows three pillars: identify the allergen, eliminate exposure, and quiet the inflammation. Allergen avoidance is the only definitive cure and should be set up before any pharmacotherapy is started. Patients leave the clinic with a written allergen avoidance plan, an ingredient-list translation aid (such as the ACDS CAMP app or the SkinSAFE database), and product substitutions verified to be free of the offending compound. Acute flares are managed with topical corticosteroids of potency matched to the site — hydrocortisone 1-2.5% on face, eyelids, and folds; triamcinolone acetonide 0.1% on body sites; clobetasol propionate 0.05% short courses for thick lichenified plaques or palmoplantar disease — applied once or twice daily for 2-4 weeks. Cool wet compresses with normal saline or aluminum acetate (Burow's solution) help oozing acute dermatitis. Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.1% ointment, pimecrolimus 1% cream) are steroid-sparing options ideal for face, eyelids, neck, and folds; topical JAK inhibitor ruxolitinib 1.5% cream and PDE-4 inhibitor crisaborole 2% ointment are additional non-steroid choices. Severe widespread acute ACD (such as bullous poison ivy or facial dermatitis after hair dye) usually requires a short course of oral corticosteroids — prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day tapered over 2-3 weeks; shorter courses produce rebound. For chronic refractory disease where avoidance is incomplete (such as occupational hand dermatitis), narrowband UVB or hand-and-foot UVA1 phototherapy, oral immunosuppressants (cyclosporine 3-5 mg/kg/day, methotrexate, azathioprine, mycophenolate), and emerging targeted systemic therapy (dupilumab is increasingly used off-label, and oral JAK inhibitors upadacitinib and abrocitinib have early evidence) are reasonable next steps. Antihistamines have a limited role: sedating agents at night may aid sleep but non-sedating antihistamines do not relieve allergic-contact itch. Antibiotics are reserved for documented bacterial superinfection. Patient education is the single highest-yield long-term intervention — repeated education plus a written avoidance plan reduces flare frequency by 40-50% in randomized trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Allergen avoidance plan with ingredient-list translation (ACDS CAMP, SkinSAFE)
medicalTreatments.description: The cornerstone of treatment. After patch testing, the dermatologist generates a personalized allergen-avoidance list and product database access. Patients learn to read INCI ingredient labels for synonyms (e.g. methylchloroisothiazolinone is also labelled Kathon CG) and choose verified alternatives.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Strict avoidance resolves the dermatitis in 60-80% of patients within 2-3 months; partial avoidance produces correspondingly partial control.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical corticosteroids (hydrocortisone 1-2.5% face/folds; triamcinolone 0.1% body; clobetasol 0.05% short courses for lichenified plaques)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line anti-inflammatory therapy. Potency is matched to site and severity. Applied once or twice daily during a flare for 2-4 weeks, then tapered. Long-term continuous high-potency use is avoided due to atrophy, telangiectasia, and steroid-induced rosacea.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clearance or near-clearance in 70-80% of mild-moderate flares within 2-4 weeks when allergen avoidance is also in place.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.1% ointment or 0.03% in children; pimecrolimus 1% cream)
medicalTreatments.description: Steroid-sparing immunomodulators ideal for face, eyelids, neck, and folds where chronic corticosteroid use causes atrophy. Apply twice daily during flares. Mild transient burning is the main side effect. Used widely in contact-allergic eyelid dermatitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Investigator Global Assessment clear or almost-clear in roughly 50-60% at 6 weeks in randomized trials of facial and eyelid dermatitis.

medicalTreatments.name: Cool wet compresses with normal saline or aluminum acetate (Burow's solution)
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunctive measure for acute oozing or vesicular dermatitis. Soaked cotton or gauze is applied to the affected skin for 15-20 minutes three to four times daily. Reduces pruritus, dries vesicles, and improves comfort during the first 3-7 days of a severe flare.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic relief in 80-90% of acute flares as an adjunct to topical or systemic anti-inflammatory therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral corticosteroids (prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day for 5-7 days, then taper over 2-3 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for severe widespread acute ACD — facial dermatitis from hair dye, bullous poison ivy, eyelid dermatitis impairing vision. Shorter courses risk rebound flare; taper over 14-21 days. Avoid in patients with uncontrolled diabetes, severe osteoporosis, or active infection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant clinical response in 80-90% within 3-5 days when paired with allergen removal.

medicalTreatments.name: Cyclosporine (3-5 mg/kg/day) for refractory chronic disease
medicalTreatments.description: Rapid-acting calcineurin inhibitor used as a bridge in severe chronic hand or generalized ACD when topical therapy fails and avoidance is incomplete. Treatment is limited to 3-6 months due to renal toxicity and hypertension; baseline and monthly creatinine and blood pressure monitoring required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Roughly 50% reduction in dermatitis severity within 6-8 weeks in randomized hand-eczema trials (Thyssen Contact Dermatitis 2022 guideline).

medicalTreatments.name: Alitretinoin (oral 9-cis retinoic acid, 30 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Licensed in Europe and Canada for severe chronic hand eczema unresponsive to topical corticosteroids. Particularly useful in hyperkeratotic and fissured palmar disease. Teratogenic — pregnancy prevention program mandatory.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clear or almost-clear hands in roughly 48% of treated patients at 24 weeks versus 17% with placebo (BACH trial, Ruzicka BJD 2008).

medicalTreatments.name: Phototherapy (narrowband UVB or local hand/foot UVA1 or PUVA)
medicalTreatments.description: Steroid-sparing option for chronic hand and foot dermatitis and widespread disease. Typically 2-3 sessions weekly for 8-16 weeks. Suppresses cutaneous T-cell responses and reduces flare frequency.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Roughly 50-60% of patients achieve 50% improvement at 12 weeks; durable response in many for several months after a completed course.

medicalTreatments.name: Dupilumab (300 mg subcutaneous every 2 weeks after 600 mg load) — off-label
medicalTreatments.description: Monoclonal antibody against the interleukin-4 receptor alpha chain. Approved for atopic dermatitis but increasingly used off-label for chronic ACD that fails standard therapy, particularly when there is overlap with atopic disease. No laboratory monitoring required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Case series and small randomized data report 50-80% improvement in chronic refractory contact dermatitis; long-term outcomes still being characterized.

medicalTreatments.name: Methotrexate (10-25 mg weekly orally or subcutaneously) or azathioprine (1-2.5 mg/kg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Steroid-sparing systemic options for chronic ACD that fails topical therapy and avoidance. Both require baseline screening (CBC, LFTs, hepatitis serology, TPMT for azathioprine) and ongoing monitoring. Avoid in pregnancy and active infection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Roughly 40-60% achieve significant improvement at 12-24 weeks in hand-eczema trials; response is slower than cyclosporine but better tolerated long-term.

alternativeTreatments.name: Barrier creams and protective gloves with cotton liners
alternativeTreatments.description: For occupational exposures that cannot be eliminated, double-glove protocols (cotton inner glove, accelerator-free nitrile outer glove) and pre-shift application of barrier creams measurably reduce flare rates in randomized occupational hand-eczema trials.

alternativeTreatments.name: Patient education programmes and written eczema action plans
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured group education and individualized written avoidance plans reduce flare frequency by 40-50% across randomized trials in chronic hand dermatitis and pediatric contact eczema (Thyssen Contact Dermatitis 2022 guideline).

alternativeTreatments.name: Habit-reversal training for chronic scratching
alternativeTreatments.description: Targets the itch-scratch cycle in chronic lichenified disease. Small but reproducible improvements in symptom scores and quality of life when paired with active treatment.

whySeeSpecialist: Refer to a dermatologist with patch-testing experience whenever the dermatitis is chronic, recurrent, occupationally disabling, fails first-line topical therapy after 4-6 weeks, or has an unusual distribution that suggests a contact pattern. Allergists are appropriate co-managers when contact urticaria or systemic allergy is also suspected, and occupational physicians should be involved early in work-related cases to address workplace exposures and disability paperwork. Patients with eyelid, facial, perianal, or genital ACD particularly benefit from early specialist input because of the diagnostic complexity and risk of treatment-induced atrophy in these sites.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a board-certified dermatologist who routinely performs patch testing with both the North American Core Series and supplemental series (cosmetics, hairdressers, dental, plastics-and-glues, fragrance, plants). Ask whether the clinic offers ROAT testing for ambiguous reactions and whether the dermatologist provides a written allergen-avoidance plan plus access to a product database such as the ACDS CAMP app. Continuity matters — allergen avoidance is a long-term skill set and a clinician who knows your trigger profile will out-perform episodic specialist visits.
preventionStrategies: Choose nickel-free jewellery (stainless steel grade 316L, titanium, gold above 18 karat, platinum) and avoid prolonged contact with belt buckles, watchbacks, and mobile-phone metal frames if you are already sensitized
Read INCI ingredient lists on cosmetics and personal-care products, and verify alternatives through ACDS CAMP or SkinSAFE before purchase if you carry a known allergy
Switch to accelerator-free nitrile gloves and use cotton liners in wet-work occupations to break the rubber-accelerator and detergent-exposure cycle
Avoid black henna temporary tattoos in children and travellers — PPD concentrations are 10-100 times those allowed in regulated hair dyes
Test new leave-on cosmetics on a small area of the antecubital fossa for 2-3 days before applying to the face or eyelids
Substitute petroleum jelly or simple bland emollients for over-the-counter triple-antibiotic creams on minor cuts — neomycin and bacitracin sensitization is a leading preventable cause of ACD
lifestyleModifications: Maintain a daily fragrance-free emollient routine to support the skin barrier — barrier breakdown amplifies allergen penetration even between flares
Apply emollient immediately after washing hands and at every shift break in wet-work occupations; carry travel-size containers
Wash new clothes before wearing and use fragrance-free, dye-free detergent; skip fabric softeners which often contain quaternary ammonium sensitizers
Replace cosmetics, shampoos, and laundry products one at a time when starting a new avoidance plan so that any flare can be traced to a specific re-introduction
Keep a flare diary noting products, occupational exposures, jewellery, and travel — patterns often emerge that identify overlooked allergens
Address comorbid atopic dermatitis with daily emollients and proactive topical anti-inflammatory maintenance — a healthier barrier reduces the rate of new contact sensitization

recommended: Balanced diet appropriate for age — no specific food group eliminations without documented systemic contact allergy
Adequate vitamin D in deficient patients — modestly supports barrier integrity in randomized trials
Whole grains, vegetables, and lean protein to support overall skin and immune health
Plenty of water to maintain hydration and reduce concomitant xerosis
Probiotic-containing fermented foods if tolerated — early data suggest modest impact on atopic comorbidities
avoid: Foods rich in balsam of Peru-related compounds (citrus peel, cinnamon, vanilla, chocolate, cola, tomatoes, spiced foods) in patients with proven systemic contact dermatitis to balsam of Peru and a clear food trigger on diary
High-nickel foods (cocoa, soy, oats, dried legumes, canned foods, whole-grain wheat) only when severe systemic nickel allergy is documented and dietitian-supervised
Broad untested elimination diets — they cause nutritional gaps without reducing dermatitis in most patients
Excess alcohol — disrupts sleep, lowers itch threshold, and may amplify systemic contact reactions to balsam-related compounds
Highly processed foods with multiple flavourings and preservatives in patients with documented fragrance or preservative systemic ACD

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is safe and encouraged. Sweat alone is irritant rather than allergic and rarely flares pure ACD, but wet skin penetrates allergens more efficiently — shower lukewarm within an hour after activity, then apply emollient. Use accelerator-free gloves for sports involving rubber grips, and wash hands after handling gym equipment, weights, and turf. Swimmers with chlorine-related dermatitis should rinse and moisturize immediately on exit.
dailyManagement: Apply a thick fragrance-free emollient at least twice daily — the highest-yield habit for sustaining barrier integrity between flares
Carry a written allergen-avoidance list and use ACDS CAMP or SkinSAFE to verify any new product before purchase
Use the fingertip unit dosing method for topical corticosteroids to avoid under- or over-treatment
Wear protective gloves with cotton liners during wet work and chemical exposure at home and at work; replace gloves before the inner liner becomes damp
Maintain a flare action plan: at the first sign of itch or redness, restart the prescribed flare regimen and tighten avoidance rather than waiting
prognosis: With confident allergen identification and strict avoidance, the prognosis for allergic contact dermatitis is excellent. Most acute flares resolve within 2-4 weeks once exposure is eliminated and topical anti-inflammatory therapy is started. Long-term clearance depends on how comprehensively the allergen can be removed from daily life — patients with nickel, fragrance, or preservative allergy who substitute products correctly remain clear in 60-80% of cases at 6-12 months. Occupational hand dermatitis is the most prognostically difficult subset: persistent disease occurs in 30-50% even with workplace modifications, and 5-10% of severely affected workers leave their occupation permanently. ACD itself does not shorten life, but chronic disease carries measurable burden — significant impact on work, daily activities, and quality-of-life scores comparable to moderate psoriasis, plus increased rates of anxiety and depression. The decisive prognostic factor is not the initial severity but how well allergen avoidance is sustained over months and years. Sensitization, once established, persists for life; a single re-exposure decades later can reproduce the original dermatitis. Patients who successfully integrate avoidance into daily life typically remain symptom-free indefinitely.
recoveryTimeline: An acute allergic contact dermatitis flare typically responds within days of allergen removal and topical corticosteroid initiation: visible redness drops within 3-5 days, itch within 5-10 days, and most flares clear within 2-4 weeks. Lichenified chronic plaques take longer — 4-12 weeks of consistent therapy plus avoidance. Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation can persist for 2-6 months after the active dermatitis resolves, especially in skin of color. After successful patch-testing and confirmed avoidance, most patients see meaningful improvement within 1-3 months, and full re-equilibration of skin barrier and microbiome over 3-6 months. Re-exposure at any point can reproduce the original eruption within 24-72 hours.
complications: Chronic lichenified, fissured hand or foot dermatitis with significant occupational disability — preventable with early diagnosis and structured avoidance
Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation or hypopigmentation, particularly in skin of color, persisting for months after active disease resolves
Bacterial superinfection (Staphylococcus aureus impetiginization or cellulitis) over excoriated or fissured skin
Eczema herpeticum — disseminated herpes simplex virus infection of dermatitic skin, a dermatologic emergency requiring IV acyclovir
Erythroderma — generalized exfoliative dermatitis, especially after systemic exposure to the allergen in a heavily sensitized patient
Loss of occupation in 5-10% of severely affected workers with refractory hand dermatitis, with significant financial and psychological consequences
Topical-corticosteroid-induced atrophy, telangiectasia, or rosacea-like reaction from prolonged misuse, particularly on the face and eyelids
Sleep disturbance, anxiety, and depression in chronic disease, with quality-of-life scores comparable to moderate psoriasis

supportResources.name: American Contact Dermatitis Society (ACDS)
supportResources.description: Patient resources, FAQs, and the CAMP database for personalized allergen-avoidance product lists.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Dermatology — Contact Dermatitis
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education from the body that publishes dermatology guidelines.

supportResources.name: DermNet — Allergic Contact Dermatitis
supportResources.description: Detailed peer-reviewed clinical and patient-facing content used by clinicians worldwide.

supportResources.name: National Eczema Association — Types of Eczema
supportResources.description: US-based patient advocacy organization with evidence-based education on contact dermatitis and related eczemas.

confusedWithConditions.name: Irritant Contact Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Irritant contact dermatitis is non-immunologic — it follows direct chemical or physical injury to the skin and develops in anyone exposed to a high enough dose, without prior sensitization. Burning and stinging dominate over itch, borders are less sharply demarcated, and patch testing is negative. ACD, by contrast, requires prior sensitization and produces a delayed (24-72 hour) itchy vesicular reaction with positive patch testing.

confusedWithConditions.name: Atopic Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Atopic dermatitis is a lifelong, flexural, itchy condition that usually starts in infancy and is accompanied by personal or family atopy (asthma, hay fever, food allergy). ACD often starts in adulthood, follows the contact pattern of the offending allergen rather than flexural creases, and is identified by patch testing. The two can coexist — patients with atopic dermatitis develop ACD at 1.5-2 fold the general-population rate.

confusedWithConditions.name: Nummular Eczema
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Nummular eczema produces coin-shaped scaly plaques on the extremities and trunk, most often in adults with dry skin, without a contact pattern or relevant exposure history. ACD has sharply demarcated borders that map to specific contact, and patch testing identifies the trigger.

confusedWithConditions.name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Seborrheic dermatitis produces greasy yellow scale on sebum-rich areas (scalp, eyebrows, nasolabial folds, chest) driven by an inflammatory response to Malassezia yeast. ACD typically lacks the greasy scale, follows a contact pattern, and improves with allergen avoidance rather than antifungals.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriasis presents as well-demarcated thick red plaques with silvery scale on extensor surfaces, scalp, and the lumbosacral area; ACD favours the contact site and produces an oozing, vesicular phase that psoriasis lacks. Nail pitting and arthritis support psoriasis; positive patch testing supports ACD.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tinea Corporis (Ringworm)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tinea corporis produces annular plaques with central clearing and an active scaly border, and KOH microscopy of scrapings reveals hyphae. ACD lacks central clearing, follows a contact pattern, and KOH is negative. The two can coexist — antifungal-treated tinea sometimes leaves ACD to a topical antifungal vehicle.

relatedConditions.name: Irritant Contact Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The other half of the contact dermatitis family; non-immunologic, dose-dependent, and frequently coexists with ACD in occupational hand disease.

relatedConditions.name: Atopic Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Filaggrin loss-of-function and active atopic dermatitis raise ACD risk 1.5-2 fold by increasing allergen penetration through a defective barrier.

relatedConditions.name: Eczema
relatedConditions.relevance: Umbrella term that encompasses ACD; readers searching 'eczema' often actually have contact dermatitis identified only after patch testing.

relatedConditions.name: Nummular Eczema
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares dry, scaly, well-demarcated plaques and is often patch-tested when the typical numular distribution is atypical or refractory.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic Contact Dermatitis due to Metals
relatedConditions.relevance: Metals — predominantly nickel, but also cobalt and chromium — are the single largest allergen category in patch-test registries worldwide.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic Contact Dermatitis due to Cosmetics
relatedConditions.relevance: Cosmetics drive most facial and eyelid ACD; fragrance and preservative allergens dominate.

faqs.question: Is allergic contact dermatitis curable?
faqs.answer: Allergic contact dermatitis is not curable in the sense that the sensitization is lifelong, but the disease is fully controllable with strict allergen avoidance. Once the allergen is identified by patch testing and removed from daily exposure, most patients remain symptom-free indefinitely. Re-exposure at any point — even decades later — can reproduce the original dermatitis within 24-72 hours, which is why ongoing avoidance is the key to long-term control.

faqs.question: How do I know if it is allergic or irritant contact dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Allergic contact dermatitis is itch-dominant, follows a 24-72 hour delay after re-exposure, requires prior sensitization, and produces sharply demarcated vesicular reactions. Irritant contact dermatitis is burning- or stinging-dominant, occurs in anyone given enough exposure without prior sensitization, has less sharply demarcated borders, and is patch-test negative. Both can coexist, so dermatology referral for patch testing is the only reliable way to tell them apart in chronic cases.

faqs.question: What is patch testing and what should I expect?
faqs.answer: Patch testing is the diagnostic gold standard for ACD. Small chambers loaded with standardized allergens are taped to the upper back for 48 hours, then read by the dermatologist at days 2, 4, and 7. The procedure is painless, takes 30-60 minutes per visit, and identifies the offending allergen in roughly 70-80% of cases. Patients keep the area dry, avoid exercise and sun, and bring their own products to test.

faqs.question: What are the most common contact allergens?
faqs.answer: The most common contact allergens worldwide are nickel (in jewellery and metal accessories), fragrance mix and balsam of Peru (in cosmetics and personal-care products), methylisothiazolinone (a preservative in liquid soaps and household cleaners), topical antibiotics neomycin and bacitracin, rubber accelerators (in gloves), p-phenylenediamine (in hair dye and black henna), and urushiol from poison ivy. The North American Contact Dermatitis Group publishes updated rankings every 2 years.

faqs.question: Can I develop a new allergy in adulthood?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sensitization can occur at any age and often develops in adulthood after prolonged or repeated exposure to a new product or workplace chemical. Common adult-onset sensitizations include hair dye, cosmetic preservatives, rubber accelerators in gloves, and topical medications used on broken skin. Once sensitization is established it is essentially lifelong.

faqs.question: How long does an ACD rash last?
faqs.answer: An acute flare typically resolves over 2-4 weeks once the allergen is removed and topical corticosteroid is started. Visible redness drops within 3-5 days, itch within 5-10 days, and full clearance follows over the next 2-3 weeks. Chronic lichenified plaques take longer — 4-12 weeks of consistent therapy plus avoidance. Continued exposure perpetuates the rash indefinitely.

faqs.question: Why does my eyelid get red after using new makeup?
faqs.answer: Eyelid skin is thin and absorbent, so it often shows allergic contact dermatitis to cosmetics, nail products transferred by the fingers, and hair products that drip onto the face. Common eyelid culprits include preservatives (methylisothiazolinone), fragrances, gold, and nickel in eyelash curlers. Patch testing identifies the trigger and a dermatologist can recommend safe alternatives.

faqs.question: Is nickel allergy common?
faqs.answer: Yes. Nickel is the single most common contact allergen worldwide. Roughly 17-19% of women and 3% of men patch-tested in the North American Contact Dermatitis Group 2019-2020 series reacted to nickel sulfate. Early ear piercing in childhood is the strongest risk factor, and once sensitization is established it persists for life.

faqs.question: Can poison ivy spread from person to person?
faqs.answer: No. The rash itself is not contagious. The urushiol oil from poison ivy, oak, or sumac is the trigger and can persist on skin, clothing, tools, and pet fur for weeks — that residual oil can transfer to another person and produce a rash, but the existing rash cannot. Wash skin, clothes, and tools with soap and water to remove urushiol.

faqs.question: Are steroid creams safe for allergic contact dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Yes, topical corticosteroids are safe and effective when used correctly. Match potency to the site (low on face and folds, mid on body, high for thick plaques), apply once or twice daily during a flare for 2-4 weeks, and taper. Long-term continuous high-potency use on the face causes atrophy and rosacea, so calcineurin inhibitors are preferred for facial and eyelid maintenance.

faqs.question: What should I do if I cannot avoid the allergen at work?
faqs.answer: Discuss workplace adjustments with your dermatologist and occupational physician. Options include substituting the allergen-containing product, using accelerator-free nitrile gloves with cotton liners, applying barrier creams pre-shift, and adjusting tasks. Where avoidance is impossible, structured topical and systemic therapy plus formal occupational disability assessment may be needed.

faqs.question: Can children get allergic contact dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. About 25-30% of patch-tested children show clinically relevant contact allergies, most often to nickel, fragrances, preservatives, and topical antibiotics. Black henna temporary tattoos applied to children can cause severe p-phenylenediamine sensitization with lifelong consequences for hair-dye use. Patch testing is well tolerated from school age onward.

faqs.question: Does diet matter for allergic contact dermatitis?
faqs.answer: For most patients, food does not drive ACD. A subset with proven systemic contact dermatitis to balsam of Peru or nickel may benefit from a targeted low-balsam or low-nickel diet under dietitian supervision when classic flare patterns are documented. Broad untested elimination diets cause nutritional gaps without reducing dermatitis.

faqs.question: What is the difference between allergic contact dermatitis and atopic dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Atopic dermatitis is a lifelong, flexural, itchy condition that usually starts in infancy with personal or family atopy. Allergic contact dermatitis often starts in adulthood, follows the contact pattern of the offending allergen, and is identified by patch testing. The two can coexist — atopic patients carry 1.5-2 fold higher risk of contact sensitization because of their disrupted skin barrier.

faqs.question: Are 'hypoallergenic' or 'natural' products safer?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. 'Hypoallergenic' and 'natural' are unregulated marketing terms — many natural and botanical products contain potent fragrance and preservative allergens, including balsam of Peru, propolis, and tea tree oil. The only reliable safety check is to read the INCI ingredient list against your personal allergen list, using the ACDS CAMP app or SkinSAFE database.

faqs.question: How much does patch testing cost?
faqs.answer: In the United States, comprehensive patch testing typically costs USD 600-1500 out of pocket and is covered by most insurance plans when ordered by a dermatologist for chronic or refractory dermatitis. In the UK and Europe, patch testing is widely available through public health systems with no direct cost. In India and other emerging markets, panel testing is increasingly available at urban dermatology centres for INR 5,000-15,000.

faqs.question: Can ACD cause a generalized rash beyond the contact site?
faqs.answer: Yes. Severe acute ACD can trigger autoeczematization (id reaction) — scattered papulovesicles on the trunk and limbs even where no allergen touched. Systemic contact dermatitis from oral or systemic exposure in a sensitized patient produces a symmetric flexural eruption known as baboon syndrome. Both resolve with allergen removal and anti-inflammatory therapy.

faqs.question: When is allergic contact dermatitis a medical emergency?
faqs.answer: Seek same-day care if you develop generalized peeling with chills and fever (erythroderma), spreading honey-coloured crusting and fever (bacterial superinfection), sudden painful clustered vesicles with fever (eczema herpeticum), or severe periorbital swelling with eye pain or vision change. These complications can progress rapidly and need prompt hospital, antiviral, or antibiotic care.

faqs.question: Can I get ACD from medications I take by mouth?
faqs.answer: Yes — this is called systemic contact dermatitis. A person already sensitized to a chemical on the skin can flare a generalized eczematous, dyshidrotic, or symmetric flexural rash when re-exposed orally or intravenously. Common triggers include metals (nickel), antibiotics, balsam-of-Peru-related compounds, and corticosteroids. Diagnosis is by exposure history plus patch testing.

faqs.question: How can I prevent allergic contact dermatitis from coming back?
faqs.answer: Identify the allergen through patch testing, build a personalized avoidance plan with your dermatologist, verify every new product against the ACDS CAMP or SkinSAFE database, and maintain a daily fragrance-free emollient routine to support the skin barrier. Use accelerator-free nitrile gloves with cotton liners for wet work, and avoid black henna temporary tattoos and over-the-counter neomycin or bacitracin ointments.

faqs.question: Does stress make allergic contact dermatitis worse?
faqs.answer: Stress does not cause ACD but is a common flare amplifier. Stress releases neuropeptides that lower the itch threshold and disrupt the barrier, while sleep loss intensifies the itch-scratch cycle. Habit-reversal training, cognitive-behavioural therapy, and sleep hygiene reduce flare frequency in chronic disease.

faqs.question: Can pregnant women treat allergic contact dermatitis safely?
faqs.answer: Yes. Daily emollients, low-to-mid potency topical corticosteroids on small areas, and narrowband UVB phototherapy are considered safe in pregnancy. Topical calcineurin inhibitors are generally avoided. Oral retinoids (alitretinoin) and oral JAK inhibitors are contraindicated. Severe flares may need a short course of systemic corticosteroid after discussion with obstetrics and dermatology.

regionalNames.name: एलर्जिक संपर्क जिल्द की सूजन (Allergic Sampark Jild ki Soojan)

metaTitle: Allergic Contact Dermatitis: Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Allergic contact dermatitis: itchy rash from nickel, fragrance, and other contact allergens. Patch testing, treatment, and avoidance reviewed by our board.

images.altText: Medical illustration of allergic contact dermatitis showing inflamed skin with vesicles and erythema after allergen exposure
images.caption: Allergic contact dermatitis — inflammatory response in the skin after re-exposure to a sensitizing allergen.

images.altText: Clinical photograph of contact dermatitis on the forearm showing erythematous papules and vesicles in a sharply demarcated pattern
images.caption: Acute contact dermatitis with sharply demarcated erythema and papulovesicles mapping to the area of allergen contact.

images.altText: Linear vesicular rash on the arm seven days after urushiol exposure from poison ivy, the textbook presentation of allergic contact dermatitis to Toxicodendron
images.caption: Urushiol-induced allergic contact dermatitis from poison ivy, 7 days after exposure — note the linear vesicular pattern from plant brush contact.

images.altText: Strong 2+ positive patch test reaction on the back showing erythema, edema, and vesicles at the allergen application site
images.caption: Strong (2+) positive patch-test reaction — erythema, edema and vesicles at the allergen chamber site, the diagnostic gold standard for allergic contact dermatitis.
